explanation: The default gateway is removed from the routing table with `route del default` (legacy net-tools) or `ip route del default` (iproute2). `ifconfig unset default` is not valid syntax, `netstat -r` only displays routes, and `sysctl ipv4.default_gw=0` is not a real mechanism for deleting a default route.

The `ifup` command brings a network interface up using distribution-specific configuration files, such as `/etc/network/interfaces` on Debian/Ubuntu or `/etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-*` on RHEL-family systems. It reads the static settings defined there rather than applying ad hoc parameters on the command line.

NetworkManager is designed to coexist with existing configuration: by default it does not alter interfaces that are already configured by the distribution's tools. It can also be configured (e.g., via `nm-connection-manager` or keyfile settings) to respect distribution network scripts. It does not forcibly switch all interfaces to DHCP, require per-interface enablement before any management, or disable non-NetworkManager interfaces.
HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL) uses TCP port 443 by IANA assignment. Port 80 is plain HTTP, port 25 is SMTP, port 8080 is a common alternate HTTP port, and port 636 is LDAP over SSL (LDAPS), not HTTPS.

`/etc/locale.conf` (systemd) sets system-wide locale variables from the standard LC_* family, including `LC_ALL` (override all categories) and `LC_TIME` (date/time formatting). `LC_USERNAME`, `LC_UTF8`, and `LC_GEOGRAPHY` are not valid locale category names.

The system timezone is set by linking `/etc/localtime` to the desired zoneinfo file: `ln -sf /usr/share/zoneinfo/Canada/Eastern /etc/localtime`. The other options use nonexistent or incorrect tools (`localegen`, `tzconf`, `modprobe tz_ca_est`) or wrong mechanisms (`sysctl clock.tz` is not how Linux sets timezones).

The `-m` option to `useradd` creates the user's home directory and copies skeleton files from `/etc/skel` into it. Without `-m`, the home directory is not created automatically unless other options or defaults apply.

Access to `at` is controlled by `/etc/at.allow` and `/etc/at.deny`. Adding a user to `/etc/at.deny` blocks them from scheduling at jobs. Listing a user in `/etc/at.allow` would permit access (when that file exists), and there is no `[deny]` section in `atd.conf` or an `atd --deny` command.

`/etc/shadow` stores the encrypted password and password-aging metadata, including the date of the last password change (days since the Unix epoch, field 3). `/etc/passwd` holds basic account info but uses `x` in the password field when shadow passwords are enabled; it does not store the change date.

Each line in `/etc/group` has the format `group_name:password:GID:member_list`, so it contains the group name and a comma-separated list of member usernames. It does not store a home directory, ACL defaults, or a free-text description for the group.

A systemd timer unit uses a `[Timer]` section to define when the associated service should be triggered (e.g., `OnCalendar`, `OnBootSec`). Sections like `[Events]`, `[cron]`, `[Schedule]`, and `[Trigger]` are not part of the timer unit file format.

`getent passwd` queries the Name Service Switch (NSS) passwd database and lists all users from `/etc/passwd` and any configured sources (LDAP, NIS, etc.). `getent homes`, `getent uids`, `getent users`, and `getent logins` are not valid database names.

The crontab fields are minute, hour, day-of-month, month, and day-of-week. `15 14 * * 1-5` runs at 14:15 (2:15 PM) on Monday through Friday. The `1-5` in the last field refers to weekdays, not months or days of the month, and the minute/hour order is not reversed.

When neither `/etc/cron.allow` nor `/etc/cron.deny` exists, only root may create user-specific crontabs by default on LPIC-tested systems. If `cron.allow` exists, only listed users may use cron; if only `cron.deny` exists, listed users are denied while others are allowed.
`iconv` converts text between character encodings (e.g., ISO-8859-1 to UTF-8). It does not convert image formats, validate FHS directory layout, change inode modes, or read `.ico` file metadata.

When shadow passwords are enabled, `/etc/passwd` shows `x` in the password field to indicate the actual encrypted password is stored in `/etc/shadow`. An asterisk (`*`) typically means the account is locked; `s`, `-`, and `#` are not the standard shadow-password placeholder.

A Braille display is an assistive hardware device that represents text as patterns of raised dots that blind users can read by touch. It is not a desktop theme, desktop environment, legacy LCD technology, or a data-exchange file format.

The `DISPLAY` environment variable tells an X11 client which X server to connect to, typically in the form `hostname:display.screen` (e.g., `:0` for the local first display). Without it, graphical clients do not know where to send their output.

A display manager (e.g., GDM, LightDM, SDDM; the question's XDM/KDM are older examples) presents the graphical login screen and starts the user's desktop session after authentication. Hot-plugging monitors, generating X11 config files, and automatic screen locking are handled by other components, not the display manager's core role.

SPICE (Simple Protocol for Independent Computing Environments) provides remote display and device access for virtual machines, including video output from the guest's graphics card. X11 is a general windowing protocol, XDMCP is for remote display-manager sessions, and KDE/Xfce are desktop environments, not VM display protocols.
